perturbation.original_text: Without the Company's prior consent, neither your or anybody acting on your behalf shall directly or indirectly for a period of two years from the date hereof (a) induce or encourage any employee of Zeigler to leave employment with Zeigler or (b) employ or hire the services of any executive, managerial, supervisory, technical, or geological employee, of Zeigler, provided that you shall not be prohibited by clause (b) above from employing or hiring the services of any person who has to be an employee of Zeigler for a period of at least 180 days prior to any direct or indirect communication of any kind between such person and you relating to possible or actual employment of such Person or hiring such Person's services.
perturbation.changed_text: Without the Company's prior consent, neither you or anybody acting on your behalf shall directly or indirectly for a period of six months from the date hereof (a) induce or encourage any employee of Zeigler to leave employment with Zeigler or (b) employ or hire the services of any executive, managerial, supervisory, technical, or geological employee, of Zeigler, provided that you shall not be prohibited by clause (b) above from employing or hiring the services of any person who has to be an employee of Zeigler for a period of at least 30 days prior to any direct or indirect communication of any kind between such person and you relating to possible or actual employment of such Person or hiring such Person's services.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the non-solicitation period to six months and the restriction on hiring former employees to 30 days creates a potential conflict with typical non-compete enforceability standards, which often require longer periods to be considered reasonable, depending on the specific role and jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Antitrust laws, including the Sherman Act (15 U.S. Code § 1), prohibit unreasonable restraints of trade. Non-compete agreements, including non-solicitation clauses, are scrutinized under this law. While the law doesn't explicitly state a maximum duration, courts often find agreements exceeding a reasonable timeframe (typically longer than six months in many contexts, especially for high-level employees) to be unenforceable as an unreasonable restraint on trade. This modification reduces the restricted periods, making the terms potentially more suspect under legal scrutiny for being insufficient to protect legitimate business interests while unduly restricting individual opportunities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 1, 26 Stat. 209 ; Aug. 17, 1937, ch. 690, title VIII, 50 Stat. 693 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. L. 94–145, § 2 , Dec. 12, 1975 , 89 Stat. 801 ; Pub. L. 101–588, § 4(a) , Nov. 16, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2880 ; Pub. L. 108–237, title II, § 215(a) , June 22, 2004 , 118 Stat. 668 .) perturbation.justification: The modification to the non-solicitation and non-compete periods, reducing them from two years and 180 days to six months and 30 days respectively, creates a potential conflict with antitrust laws, specifically the Sherman Act (15 U.S. Code § 1), which prohibits unreasonable restraints of trade. The Law Explanation specifies that agreements exceeding a reasonable timeframe are often deemed unenforceable. The scraped law snippet, 15 U.S. Code § 1, states that 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal.' While the statute itself doesn't define reasonableness, the reduced periods, as indicated in the Perturbation Explanation, make the terms more suspect and potentially insufficient to protect legitimate business interests, thus creating a contradiction by potentially violating the restraint of trade principle.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company's request, you shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such material, and shall certify the destruction of such materials in writing to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's request, you shall deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials within 90 days of the request, retaining copies as you see fit, and without any requirement to certify the destruction of such materials to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the return/destruction clause to allow for a 90-day period and retention of copies contradicts common legal requirements for prompt return of confidential information and certification of destruction in confidentiality agreements. It implies a lack of urgency and control over sensitive materials.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by weakening the obligation to protect trade secrets, potentially violating the Economic Espionage Act (18 U.S. Code § 1832). This act criminalizes the misappropriation of trade secrets. By allowing a 90-day window for returning or destroying materials and permitting the retention of copies, the modified text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure or use of confidential information, which could lead to a violation of the duty to protect trade secrets under federal law. The removal of the certification requirement further diminishes accountability and control over the destruction process.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret , that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret , knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains such information; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys such information; (3) receives, buys, or possesses such information, knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 10 years, or both. (b) Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $5,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3489 ; amended Pub. L. 112–236, § 2 , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1627 ; Pub. L. 114–153, § 3(a)(1) , May 11, 2016 , 130 Stat. 382

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the spirit and potential enforcement of trade secret protection, specifically regarding the safeguarding of confidential information. The original contract mandates the prompt return or destruction of evaluation materials, coupled with a certification of destruction. The changed text introduces a 90-day delay and allows for the retention of copies, removing the certification requirement. This directly undermines the urgency and verifiable accountability for the proper handling of sensitive trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 U.S. Code § 1832) outlines that 'Whoever... without authorization copies, duplicates... or conveys such information' may be in violation of trade secret laws. By explicitly permitting the retention of copies, the modified contract directly conflicts with the intent of this law, as it increases the risk of unauthorized use or disclosure. Furthermore, the absence of a certification requirement removes a crucial mechanism for ensuring and verifying compliance, further weakening the protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: LEGAL REMEDY You understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: LEGAL REMEDY You understand and agree that money damages would be the sole and exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives, superseding any rights to specific performance or injunctive relief. The maximum liability under this agreement will be capped at $1000.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the remedy to money damages and capping liability contradicts the common practice of including specific performance or injunctive relief as remedies for breaches of confidentiality agreements, especially where monetary damages may be inadequate to address the harm caused by disclosure of sensitive information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Limitations on Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification attempts to limit remedies for a breach, potentially running afoul of UCC § 2-719 (though this agreement isn't directly a sale of goods, principles apply). While parties can generally limit or exclude consequential damages or remedies, the UCC states that if a remedy fails its essential purpose, it may be unenforceable. Capping liability at $1000 and excluding equitable remedies like specific performance might be deemed unconscionable or fail the essential purpose if a breach causes significant, irreparable harm (e.g., disclosure of critical trade secrets). This is especially true if monetary damages are insufficient to compensate the injured party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original agreement by limiting the remedies available to the Company in case of a breach. The original text allows for specific performance and injunctive relief, while the changed text restricts the remedy to money damages capped at $1000. UCC § 2-719, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that agreements can provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided. However, it also states that where an exclusive or limited remedy fails of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in the Act. Capping liability at $1000 and excluding equitable remedies might be deemed unconscionable if a breach causes significant irreparable harm.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from New York to California could be problematic if the agreement contains clauses (like non-competes) that are permissible under New York law but significantly restricted or outright prohibited under California law. This may impact the enforceability of key provisions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 voids contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. California is far more restrictive on non-compete agreements than New York. If the agreement contains broad non-compete provisions, changing the governing law to California makes them almost certainly unenforceable, directly contradicting the parties' original intent and the enforceability they likely believed they had under New York law. This would not impact the entire document, but could lead to parts of it being unenforceable based on the change of governing law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change of governing law from New York to California introduces a legal contradiction because California law, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 (BPC 16600), voids contracts that restrain someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The Law Explanation states that California is far more restrictive on non-compete agreements than New York. If the agreement contains broad non-compete provisions, changing the governing law to California makes them almost certainly unenforceable. This directly contradicts the parties' original intent and the enforceability they likely believed they had under New York law. Therefore, this creates a significant legal contradiction.
